entry.title: Can I overlap production orders? (Not operations)
entry.content:
Good Afternoon,

Is there a way, from a capacity planning perspective, to overlap production orders?

At the moment this is what appears to happen:

Part A on Prod Ord 1 for qty 10, takes 5 days to make, and is required to make Part B.

Part B on Prod Ord 2 for qty 10 takes 10 days to make.

SAP will say the process to make 10x Part B (including the Part A sub assembly) will take 15 days

What I want to see is SAP telling me that instead of 15 days, this would take 10.5 days, because after the first half day I would have completed 1 Part A, which then allows be to start building part B.

Is this at all possible??

entry.title: Re: API REST integration (new UI)
entry.content:
Hello there, 

Just wanted to jump also to this topic as the new UI was causing me some headache as well when it came out. I didn't bother too much with it, rather just duplicated some old REST API resources, and could keep on with those. 

But I did find some pretty good use of it in finally being able to implement `PATCH` requests to a Xano backend. 

Some useful thoughts could potentially be: 
1. for simple query parameter setting you can use the `APPEND_URL_PARAMETERS()` formula to easily combine an object or variable into a GET query string. 

2. to ensure that your backend (in our case Xano) can identify the content of a POST/PUT/PATCH request, we found it useful to also wrap the request body into an `ENCODE_JSON()` formula. 

3. this is something I plan on exploring a little bit more: it seems that this new UI also enables the REST api to be used with Data Components (even including pagination)

I honestly hope that the UI can get slightly better and there can be more documentation about this kind of implementation, but until then this page helped me a lot: 
SAP BTP REST direct integration as it seems that the new universal REST API integration is just an SAP BTP REST integration, without predefined routes. Also I presume that as we can slowly notice other significant UI changes, I presume that the REST api integration also goes into this direction: SAP Build Help centre

entry.title: Re: The Required Functional modules Knowledge.
entry.content:
Hi @Sadory 

In general, there are four Modules that have repeated during my developer career: Material Management, Procurement, Sales and Distribution and Finance.

For MM: I suggest to have an overall understanding of Materials, Also understand the terms Plants and Storage Locations.

For Procurement: Have an overall understanding of Purchase Orders, Inbound Deliveries and Service Entry Sheets.

For SD: Have an overall understanding of Sales Orders, Outbound Deliveries and Invoices. Also understand the terms Sales Organization and Distribution Channel.

For FI: Have an overall understanding of Billing Documents, Payments and Validations/Substitutions. Also understand the terms Company Code, Account, Cost Center.

Of course, every module is made of tons of other terms, but in my case, understanding the terms I mentioned above helped me to better communicate with Functional Consultants.

entry.title: Re: MDG DQM Validation Rules – Access additional data model tables without ABAP coding
entry.content:
Hello,

it is important to note that the change request and the process differ in the fields that are filled.

The components Process ID, Step Number and Source System are only filled if the processing takes place within a process. This is the case with the Evaluate Data Quality process.

The Source ID is filled in both the change request and the process. As can be seen in your screenshots, it is filled in both cases. With the change request, the primary key of the object currently being processed is filled here. For the BP, this is the BP number.

Best regards
Stefan Weingand

entry.title: Re: Anticipo Proveedor con OC de Activo Fijo
entry.content:
Hi SFerrand,

Unfortunately,  it is impossible to post a downpayment with a purchase order associated with a Fixed asset in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

You can create an Investment Project (WBS) and then the purchase order with the AccClassCategory Projects.  

In the purchase order, you can add the values of the downpayment

And create the downpayment request with the Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments app

You must run the whole purchase order cycle and settle the project to the final fixed asset.

SAP Note 3154459 3154459 - AA834 / F5378 / SFIN_FI017 when posting a downpayment to an asset SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best regards

 

Edna

entry.title: Re: UKM_BP error R1 206 when BP Role is not the standard...
entry.content:
Read 3502521 - Error in transaction UKM_BP: "BP role UKM000 does not exist" (message number: R1206) 

Basically this role is hard-coded in the transaction 😒 actually you are calling BP with some hidden parameter id in memory.

 

REPORT  ukm_bp_start.

PARAMETERS p_id TYPE bu_partner NO-DISPLAY.

DATA:
  lc_role    LIKE bus_joel_main-partner_role VALUE 'UKM000',
  tcod       LIKE sy-tcode VALUE 'BP',
  lv_partner TYPE bu_partner.

 

Did you try to create a Z-transaction changing the default value for role in the wrapper report ?

entry.title: SAP-CRM V7.13 (non-HANA): Divide data into 2 clients to support a carve out
entry.content: Currently one client (100) consists of data of 2 divisions. That should change: Every division should become an own client (Client 100 and 200 on the same system). I have the idea to simply create a second Client 200 and go through all datatables (Masterdata and transactions), starting with BUT000 and COMM_PRODUCT and simply change the Clientnumber to 200 for the data of the carved out division via SQL? It appears to be just a minor effort to cascade down from BUT000 and COMM_PRODUCT to related masterdatatables and transactiontables. The result would be that within our database-tables we would have data of the 2 clients. Are there any concerns of doing that? To me it appears the simplest way, just simple updates via DBACockpit, no deletes. I have the impression that consulting companies and also SAP are not recommending that and instead prefer downloads and uploads, sometime via Excel, which leads to serious issues getting the correct state of transactions and moving history data. I also see some challenges related to the datamodel, like CRMD_LINK... and the integration to ERP, but that is far less critical to me than any other approach. Does anyone have experience with that approach?

entry.title: Re: Raising the production order quantity and releasing w...
entry.content:
Hi, see below some options you can evaluate:

- Increasing the Build Quantity for Overproduction manually in Manage Orders app and release more SFCs/quantities if Tolerances % are set from ERP, which is a manual option and might not be a good fit to your use case.

- SFC Split, where Allow Overproduction flag is available, so via Production Process you should be able to split the existing SFC into new child SFCs with extra quantity for every new overproduction confirmation that comes in. Example:

- Set SFC Quantity, where you can change quantity at SFC level directly. As a remark, only New and In Queue SFCs can have the quantity updated, so if the SFC is In Work in your case, you would need to SignOff the SFC first before setting the SFC quantity via API and then start it again via Production Process. Example:

Hope it helps! Br,
Manoel

entry.title: Re: Could not create instance under may space under Subac...
entry.content:
You created your HANA Cloud for "Other Environments". Normally, you would be able to add an instance mapping to Cloud Foundry but that doesn't work on BTP Trial.

To create HDI containers in your Cloud Foundry space, you'll need to delete your HANA Cloud instance and recreate it in the Cloud Foundry environment. Then, go to your trial subaccount and create a subscription to SAP HANA Cloud and choose the plan "tools" (see screenshot).

 Once the subscription has been created, go to Security -> Role Rollections and assign yourself the roles SAP HANA Cloud Administrator and SAP HANA Cloud Security Administrator.

Then go to the SAP HANA Cloud Central (the tools plan that you've just subscribed to). There, you can create a HANA Cloud instance and choose Cloud Foundry as Runtime Environment:

 Select your space, create it and all wil be as expected.

entry.title: Re: Generating firmed Planned orders
entry.content:
Good day.

Sounds like you have the firming time fence set on these particular items. This means someone has set the planning time fence and planning cycle (MRP1). It is working with the planning calendar (MRP2) and all other MRP trigger settings placed in the MRP tabs of the material master. If you set the planning time fence and the planning cycle, MRP will automatically firm all planned procurement orders during that time frame indicated. Remove the planning time fence entry and the planning cycle entry and orders will no longer come through in firmed status.

entry.title: Rebates (Condition Contracts) Partial Settlements with individual business volume
entry.content:
Partial Settlement refers to the process of making a partial payment or credit settlement against a rebate agreement. This typically occurs when a customer or business partner earns a rebate, but the full amount due under the agreement isn't paid in one go. Instead, a portion of the rebate is settled, leaving the remainder for future settlement. As an example, an annual rebate agreement with partial settlement every quarter.

Let us under how the partial settlement of rebate condition contract works in S4 with an example:

There is an agreement with the customer stipulating that they will receive a 5% rebate on their business volume, provided they make purchases totaling $100,000.

 

Quarterly Sales

Rebate amount

Q1

$50,000

0

Q2

$75,000

$6,250

Q3

$100,000

$5,000

Q4

$75,000

$3,750

 

Let’s walk through how the rebate amount is calculated each quarter. In Q1, the total sales amount is $50,000, which does not meet the required business volume of $100,000. Therefore, the rebate for Q1 is zero. By the end of Q2, the cumulative business volume reaches $125,000 (Q1: $50,000 + Q2: $75,000). The rebate is calculated on the total of $125,000, resulting in a rebate of $6,250. By the end of Q3, the total business volume increases to $225,000. A 5% rebate on this amount equals $11,250. However, since $6,250 has already been settled in Q2, this amount is deducted from the $11,250, leaving a Q3 settlement amount of $5,000.

This process follows the standard functionality in the CCM rebate agreements.

Individual Partial Settlement:

Let's examine how individual partial settlements operate. Business volumes are not cumulative; instead, each quarter's business volume is assessed independently. According to the annual agreement, the customer must purchase products worth $100,000 within a quarter to qualify for the rebate for that specific quarter

 

Quarterly Sales

Rebate amount

Q1

$50,000

0

Q2

$75,000

0

Q3

$100,000

$5,000

Q4

$75,000

0

 

Configuration required:

Menu path: WLFA -> Condition Contract Management -> Condition Contract Settlement -> Specify Settlement Settings for Condition Contract Types

In the “Settlement Dates” section, for Partial settlement type select 2-Separate.

entry.title: SAP EWM Intercompany STO Process Between 2 EWM Managed Warehouses Using SPED
entry.content:
Introduction:

The Document will discuss the STO process between 2 EWM-based warehouses belonging to 2 different company codes. The process talks about the steps and configurations that are required to successfully run the process in the S/4 Managed EWM system. Since there are various documents available where movement types 643 and 101 are used to do this process hence we will be dealing with valuated stock i.e. the goods issue will be done via movement type 683 and the goods receipt will be done via movement type 107 and 109. We will also be automating the process of Inbound delivery via SPED output type.

One of the most important points to note here is that as we are using cross-delivery HUs hence the HU’s will be transferred from the outbound delivery to the inbound delivery if the receiving warehouse is also managed by an EWM system. 

However, this process does not work if you have activated a unique number assignment for HUs. As a best practice whenever you have multiple EWM systems in your landscape as a good practice the HU number range should be different in both warehouses.

Please read more FAQ about this in SAP note 1119073 

 

STO FLOW:

 

  1. A Purchase Order is created to move stock between two EWM-managed warehouses.
  2. Outbound delivery is generated via VL10B.
  3. Outbound delivery is distributed to EWM Automatically.
  4. Picking is done via /SCWM/PRDO or RF Device.
  5. Goods issue is reflected back in outbound delivery in S/4 landscape by creating Material document with movement type 683 and 107.
  6. SPED output type is created and processed to create inbound delivery for receiving of stock.
  7. HU which were generated during repacking are attached to inbound delivery.
  8. Inbound Delivery is gets distributed in the EWM system.
  9. Goods receipt and putaway is done in the EWM system via /SCWM/PRDI or RF Device.
  10. Goods receipt is reflected in S/4 Landscape by creating a Material document with movement type 109.

 

 


Pre-Requisites for Intercompany STO Process:

  • The HU Number range should be unique in both the warehouses.
  • The HU Number range of the 1st warehouse should be maintained as the external number range in the 2nd warehouse.
  • The HU Number range of the 2nd warehouse should be maintained as the external number range in the 1st warehouse.

 

Configuration Required:

Define Shipping Data for Plants:

SPRO –> IMG –> Material Management –> Purchasing –> Purchase order –> Set up Stock Transport order –> Define Shipping Data for Plants

 

 

Configure Delivery Type & Availability Check Procedure by Plant:

SPRO –> IMG –> Material Management –> Purchasing –> Purchase order –> Set up Stock Transport order –> Assign Delivery Type and Checking Rule

 

Assign Document Type, One-Step Procedure, under delivery Tolerance:

SPRO –> IMG –> Material Management –> Purchasing –> Purchase order –> Set up Stock Transport order –> Assign Document Type, One-Step Procedure, Under delivery Tolerance

 

Define Confirmation Control:

Check the confirmation control key 0004 (Inbound Delivery) in Customizing for Materials Management, under Purchasing  Confirmations  Set Up Confirmation Control.

Under Confirmation Sequence, the two checkboxes GR-Relevant and GR with Confirmation must be set.

Set Up Condition Type SPED:

Check output determination procedure V10000 (Header Output) in Customizing for Logistics Execution, under Shipping  Basic Shipping Functions  Output Control  Output Determination  Maintain Output Determination for Outbound Deliveries  Maintain Output Determination Procedure.

Under Control Data, there must be an entry with condition type SPED (Stock Transfer Generates Inbound Delivery) and condition 408 (Stock Transfer Create ID).

Define Delivery type:

Check confirmation category LA (Inbound Delivery) in Customizing for Materials Management, under Purchasing  Confirmations  Define Internal Confirmation Categories.

The Delivery Type Inbound Delivery Stock in Transit field must contain an inbound delivery type for the process with stock in transit. We will be using the standard delivery type ELST (Inbound Delivery Receiving Valuated Stock in Transit). 

This delivery type must use an item category that uses movement type 109 (Goods Receipt from Valuated Goods Receipt Blocked Stock) or another movement type with posting string reference 109.

 

Define Item Categories for Delivery (Inbound):

In Customizing for Logistics Execution, under Shipping in the Customizing for Deliveries use the activity Define Item Categories to Deliveries, ensure that movement type 109 or a movement type with posting string reference 109 is assigned to this item category.

 

 

In Customizing for Logistics Execution, in the Customizing activity Define Item Category Determination in Deliveries, and check the item category that is assigned to this delivery type.

 

 

 

Define Item Categories for Delivery (Outbound):

As per above mentioned configuration we are using delivery type NCCR for triggering goods issue via 683 movement type

 

Hence based on item category determination NCCR delivery item is configured to be determined.

 

Once the item category is determined relevant Schedule line category is determined 

 

Based on the movement type maintained in the schedule line category, the goods issue will be triggered.

 

Setup condition record for SPED:

The system creates inbound deliveries with this item category when processing output type SPED during goods issued for an outbound delivery.

In transaction VV22 (Change Output - Condition Records: Shipping), ensure that in output determination for output type SPED, the condition records for the outbound delivery type used (NCCR, NCC3, NLIR, or NLI3) have been created as follows:

Output type SPED with role WE (Goods Recipient) or SH (Ship to Party), medium 8 (Special Function), and time 4 (Send Immediately (After Saving Application))

 

 

Process Flow Diagram:

 

 

Testing:

1. A Purchase Order is created to move stock between two EWM-managed warehouses

 

 

2. Outbound delivery is generated via VL10B

 

 

 

 

3. Outbound delivery is distributed to EWM Automatically

 

4. Warehouse Task Created

 

 

5. Warehouse Tasks are confirmed to complete the picking process

 

 

6. Goods issue posted in EWM

 

 

7. Goods issue is reflected back in outbound delivery in S/4 landscape by creating Material document with movement type 683 and 107.

 

8. HU information updated at the S/4 level

 

9. SPED output type is created and processed to create inbound delivery for receiving of stock.

 

10. Inbound delivery is created automatically and reflected in Purchase order

 

11. HU which were generated during repacking are attached to inbound delivery

12. Inbound Delivery gets distributed in the EWM system automatically.

 

13. Goods receipt and putaway is done in EWM system via /SCWM/PRDI or RF Device

14. Goods receipt is reflected in S/4 Landscape by creating a Material document with movement type 109

 

This completes the whole end to end process of intercompany STO between 2 EWM ,managed warehouses.

 

Regards,
Kamlesh Rampal.

entry.title: Automating Rate Changes in SAP Fieldglass
entry.content:
Have you ever wondered if and how you can automate changes to a worker’s pay rate or to a supplier’s bill rate over the course of an assignment? 

Are there other better options than creating manual Work Order revisions? 

There are lots of reasons why rate changes may be needed but some of the most typical use cases include: 

  • Tenure discounts where a supplier’s margin is reduced after 1 year, for example 
  • Pay parity regulations that dictate increases to a worker’s pay rate over time, e.g., collective bargaining agreements (CBAs) such as ‘Branchenzuschläge’ (can you say the word?) in Germany 

Both of these examples require rate changes that follow defined logic and that also should be taken into account when calculating spend – especially when you are creating Purchase Requisitons and Purchase Orders on the back of your Fieldglass Work Orders. 

Wouldn’t it be great if these rate changes, and the logic that defines them, could be built into your SAP Fieldglass solution so that they are applied automatically, 

  1. at the right time 
  2. at the correct rate 
  3. defined for the entire duration of your Work Order and included into the Committed Spend calculation so that you can create one, and only one, correct Purchase Order in your ERP? 

What if you could define these rate changes from the buyer side to enforce tenure discounts you otherwise may not get from your suppliers? 

What if you could have peace of mind that collective bargaining agreements, and the worker pay rate increases they dictate, are applied correctly, automatically every time? 

Well guess what, you can! SAP Fieldglass has a rate automation feature that can be used on your contingent Work Orders – well actually, it has two! Buyer-defined Rate Change Matrix and Supplier-defined Rate Change Matrix can be leveraged to do just that. 

Both versions of Rate Change Matrix can be enabled on your Job Posting Templates under SAP Fieldglass’s traditional rate model – also referred to “Rates 1.0”. 

 Supplier-defined Rate Change Matrix is the simpler version of the two. Here the supplier needs to enter the rate change(s) when submitting a Job Seeker.  

You, as the buyer, can determine on your Job Posting Template if the supplier can calculate the target (pay or bill) rate manually by entering a distinct value that the rate should change to, or by entering a percentage increase or decrease instead. 

Same goes for the timing or frequency of the rate change – do you want your suppliers to manually enter a date for the change or do you want them to enter a “schedule” after which the change or changes occur? 

Rate Change Type and Rate Change Frequency settings on your Job Posting Templates can be used for that. 

Suppliers can also create Rate Change Matrix templates in their SAP Fieldglass supplier company admin menu to define these rate changes so that they don’t need to be manually entered every time they submit a  Job Seeker. 

What these supplier-defined options have in common is that they: 

  • Can be implemented with little configuration effort on the buyer side 
  • Do not define and enforce rate changes from the buyer side 

In other words, the benefit of the Supplier-defined Rate Change Matrix  from a buyer perspective is that it is fairly low-touch but you are still reliant on your suppliers doing the right thing by submitting correct information for your tenure discount or CBA dictated pay rate increases. 

And then there is the “buyer-defined” option of your Rate Change Type. This one is the deluxe version of the available rate change automation options in SAP Fieldglass. 

 

 Buyer-defined Rate Change Matrix can be used to automate and enforce rate changes, making sure that tenure discounts are correctly applied, or worker’s pay rates comply with legal and CBA regulations at all times. 

With a Buyer-defined Rate Change Matrix, rate change logic can leverage:   

  • Custom fields where you define, for example, an equal pay pay rate for your internal employees performing the same job as a temporary worker 
  • Custom lookups where you store publicly available pay rates per pay grade level from a CBA, together with their applicable validity dates or supplier markups you have individually agreed with your suppliers 
  • Custom business rules to calculate worker pay rates and/or supplier bill rates and how/when they change throughout the course of an assignment 

The buyer-defined Rate Change Matrix is more complex to set up and it requires the involvement of SAP Fieldglass technical resources. However, it is more robust in the sense that rate change logic is defined and enforced from the buyer side, and you do not need to depend on your suppliers to submit correct rate change information. 

For more information, check out the SAP Fieldglass Help Portal, contact your Customer Success Partner / Program Success Manager, or submit a case through SAP for Me or the SAP Fieldglass Help Center.

entry.title: Is BusinessObjects Still Relevant in 2025?
entry.content:
In a world full of alternatives, is the SAP BusinessObjects platform still relevant to the enterprise? The answer is definitely “yes”, if for no other reason that SAP has invested in at least two more releases for the platform. Let’s quickly review what SAP BusinessObjects has going for it. In June 2022, the winds of change blew into the sails of the SAP BusinessObjects platform and BI 2025 was announced. In fact, it’s supposed to be delivered to us in a few short weeks. And last year, SAP committed to BI 2027, and BI 2029 is to be confirmed in H1.

Robust Server Architecture

What you call “vintage” I might call “proven”. The team that created the distributed architecture and Crystal Management Console admin tool for Crystal Enterprise many years ago should be pleased that this foundation and the rechristened Central Management Console are still largely at the heart of the BusinessObjects platform.

Operating System support

While SAP BusinessObjects supports fewer operating systems today than it has in the past, it’s a reflection of where the market has gone. The two operating systems that matter in 2025, Microsoft Windows Server and Linux, are both supported.

Cloud or On-premise?

Yes and yes. “Cloud” for many organizations now means their cloud in AWS, Azure, or GCP. Cloud vendors fully supported by SAP. And BusinessObjects can be hosted in SAP’s private cloud, for those customers who wish it.

Modern database connectivity

Take a look at the BI 4.3 platform availability matrix (PAM) and you’ll see the latest cloud-based databases like Snowflake, Databricks, AWS Redshift, Starburst, and Trino.

Powerful scheduling and bursting

Scheduling and bursting aren’t new features. But they can be lacking in some of the more “modern” analytics tools in the marketplace.

Robust semantic layer

The almighty BusinessObjects universe. The semantic layer is where many so-called modern tools fall short, with customers frequently resorting to using Microsoft Excel as database middleware.

Enterprise-Class Administration

If your organization is evaluating other tools, you’ll want to compare its administrator features against the Central Management Console. Make sure that something that performed adequately for a small number of POC users is ready to scale to the enterprise.

Modern User Experience

While desktop software was ironically central to a “land and expand” strategy for at least one of SAP’s analytic competitors, SAP provided a migration path from Desktop Intelligence many years ago. Meanwhile, having landed and expanded, those same competitors are furiously working to bring full-parity to their web-based design tools. And boasting “new” features such as tables because- news flash- enterprise reporting is still a core requirement after all.

And while browser plug-ins like Java and Adobe Flash served well in their time, they are nowhere to be found in the current BI 4.3 or the modern browsers SAP supports. SAP has provided us with a modern SAP Fiori inspired user experience.

New Features? Yeah, We’ve Got ‘Em

See Data Mode in SAP BI 4.3 SP4. Outdated systems do not add data cleansing features (or any features for that matter) to their core product.

Beware of FUD

FUD, or fear, uncertainty, and doubt, is a common marketing strategy. But be wary of the sales pitches. And demand proof and case studies to support vague claims.

BusinessObjects is an “Outdated System”

False. With frequent product updates and patches, SAP continues to modernize BusinessObjects for new browsers, operating systems, databases, and cloud computing. Let’s be clear here, “outdated systems” are operated by organizations who terminated SAP support and are nursing along legacy BI 4.1 or BI 4.2 without the benefit of patches for security issues or even simple matters like browser compatibility.

But We Have a Modern Cloud Architecture…

Ironically, with the advent of cloud computing, any software provider’s architecture is a black box. It doesn’t matter whether the architecture is a containerized warp drive or a data center filled with hundreds of clustered Raspberry PI – the value provided outside the black box is what truly matters.

Rising Costs?

The claim that staying on “outdated software” leads to rising costs demands proof. Migrating "outdated" analytics content to other tools only for the satisfaction of unplugging BusinessObjects is not bringing value to the business and brings its own budget busting challenges. Do you know what really raises costs? Software vendors using their “poster boy” advantage to muscle through price increases. Do the math, as small prudent investments in your BusinessObjects platform can bring much-needed innovation to your organization without the much larger cost of acquiring and rolling out new software.

What Next?

Technology doesn’t exist for technology’s sake. It exists to improve the success of the organization. Nothing lasts forever, but organizations that depend on BusinessObjects can continue do so for the foreseeable future with confidence.

What are your thoughts about the relevance of BusinessObjects in 2025? Share your comments below.

entry.title: Re: Rebates (Condition Contracts) Partial Settlements with individual business volume
entry.content:
Hi @HariBoyapati2,

Thank you for sharing this blog! Content on SAP CCM/Settlement Management is indeed rare and highly appreciated.

I’d like to point out a clarification regarding the Partial Settlement type set to "2-Separate":

This configuration applies specifically to partial settlements. However, the Final Settlement always evaluates the overall business volume across the entire contract validity period. This ensures that any missed transactions or discrepancies are accounted for.

In your example, the settlement for Q4 is actually a Final Settlement. As such, it will calculate the rebate as 5% of the total business volume for the year (i.e., $300,000) and deduct the amounts already settled in previous quarters:
$15,000 (5% of $300,000) - $5,000 (settled in Q3) = $10,000

Thus, the Final Settlement Amount for Q4 would be $10,000.

I hope this provides clarity. Feel free to share your thoughts!


Regards, 
Gauthier Victor

entry.title: SAP CAP Lessons Learned: Quick deployment with manifest and cf push
entry.content:
Good day fellow CAPpers,

Today I'll share one of the lessons I've learned working with CAP, something that may not be clear in the current tutorials or documentation. Whenever I create a CAP app, there's always something that doesn't quite work on BTP, or behaves slightly differently. Therefore, I frequently deploy the app to check it out.

First off, most of these issues can quickly be resolved by setting up an a Hybrid Development, which I highly recommend.

Still, there may be left a few issues that only happen when the app is running on SAP BTP. To speed up this development testing, you can create a manifest.yml file and deploy only your service (/srv) :

 

---
applications:
- name: my-app-srv
  random-route: true  # for development only
  path: gen/srv
  memory: 256M
  buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
  services:
  - my-app-auth
  - my-app-destination-service
# - my-app-connectivity
# - my-app-other-service

 

You may also use the cds add cf-manifest command to quickly generate this file.

Then, every time you make a change on your service, run:

  • cds build --production​
  • cf push

The first command will update the files on gen/srv with the latest changes and the second will push to the Cloud Foundry environment.

 

Notes:

  • This will only send changes in your service, the srv folder. Other database or UI changes will not go together.
  • It may also reset or skip usual build tasks. In such cases, you need to do the complete MTA build and deploy.

 

May this post help you on your troubleshooting. Reply if you find any issues so I, and the community, can help.

Lucas Magrini Rigo

entry.title: Everything About SAP Idocs in S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Everything About Idocs

IDocs are used in most SAP applications for transfer of message(information) from SAP system to other systems and vice versa.

 

IDOC is simply a data container used to exchange information between any two processes that can understand the syntax and semantics of the data.

In other words, an IDOC is like a data file with a specified format which is exchanged between 2 systems which know how to interpret that data.

IDOC stands for ” Intermediate Document”

When we execute an outbound ALE or EDI Process, an IDOC is created.

In the SAP System, I DOCs are stored in database. Every IDOC has a unique number(within a client).

 

 

 

 

The I doc structure consists of 3 parts –

  1. The administration part(Control Record)- which has the type of idoc, message type, the current status, the sender, receiver etc. This is referred to as the Control record.
  2. The application data (Data Record) – Which contains the data. These are called the data records/segments.
  3. The Status information (Status Record)- These give you information about the various stages the idoc has passed through.

You can view an I-DOC using transaction WE02 or WE05

 

 

 

As seen the screenshot above IDOC record has three parts Control, Data and Status.

 

Control Record

  • All control record data is stored in EDIDC table. The key to this table is the IDOC Number
  • It contains information like IDOC number, the direction(inbound/outbound), sender, recipient information, channel it is using, which port it is using etc.
  • Direction ‘1’ indicates outbound, ‘2’ indicates inbound.

Data Record

  • Data record contains application data like employee header info, weekly details, client details etc
  • All data record data is stored in EDID2 to EDID4 tables and EDIDD is a structure where you can see its components.
  • It contains data like the idoc number, name and number of the segment in the idoc, the hierarchy and the data
  • The actual data is stored as a string in a field called SDATA, which is a 1000 char long field.

Status Record

  • Status record is attached to an I-DOC at every milestone or when it encounter errors.
  • All status record data is stored in EDIDS table.
  • Statuses 1-50 are for outbound while 50-75 for inbound

 

 

 

outbound IDoc

 

The initial status of an outbound IDoc is 01 and successful status is 30 or 03.

01: IDoc generation successful

30: IDoc is ready to be processed by IDoc Processing job

03: IDoc data is passed to the Port

18: IDoc successfully triggered EDI subsystem

06: IDoc data translated to EDI format

12: IDoc is dispatched successfully to the partner

16: Partner has received the IDoc successfully

 

 

INBOUND IDOC

The initial status of an inbound IDoc is 64 and successful status is 53.

Different validation steps for inbound IDocs are explained below:

50: IDoc received successfully in the system

64: IDoc is ready to be processed by IDoc processing job

53: Application document created and saved successfully. The document number can be found by expanding the status node 53

 

REPROCESSING IDOCS

On the basis of IDoc statuses different programs can be used for reprocessing of failed IDocs. These are given below:

 

 

 

 

The SAP IDoc status describes the state of an IDoc at a defined time.

The status for outgoing IDocs has a value range between 01 and 49. The status for incoming IDocs starts at 50.

 

IDOC Types

An I DOC Type, (Basic) defines the structure and format of the business document that is to be exchanged. An IDOC is an instance of an IDOC Type , just like the concept of variables and variables types in programming languages. You can define IDOC types using WE30

What is a Segment?

A Segment defines the format and structure of a data record in I-DOC. Segments are reusable components.

For each segment SAP creates

  • Segment Type
  • Segment Definition
  • Segment Documentation

The last 3 characters is the version of the segment

Definitions keep changing as per the version but the segment type remains the same.

Transaction:WE31

 

 

Documentation

Each IDOC are thoroughly documented in transaction WE60

 

Message Type

A message represents a specific type of document that is transmitted between two partners Ex. Orders, orders responses, invoices etc

 

The message type describes the type of business document in an SAP system – e.g., an ORDERS IDoc message type is used to describe — as the name already indicates — an order document. A message type can support different use cases. The message type ORDERS for example can be used for an outbound order (sent to a supplier) or an inbound order (received from a customer).

An idoc type can be associated with many message types

Also, a message type can be associated with different idoc types. Transaction WE81

 

Partner Profiles

A partner is defined as a business partner with whom you conduct business and exchange documents

In the partner profile of a partner that we exchange Idocs with, we maintain the parameters that are necessary for exchanging the data. The transaction used is WE20.

 

Port

The port defines the technical characteristics of the connection between your SAP system and the other system you want to transfer data with (subsystem). The port defines the medium in which data is exchanged between the 2 systems.

There are different types of ports. The 2 most commonly used are the TRFC ports used in ALE and File ports which EDI uses.

For TRFC ports we have to give the name of the logical destination created using SM59.

 

IDoc basic type

The IDoc basic type provides the structure for the logical message type. The following image shows the structure of in transaction WE19..

 

The IDoc basic type can manifest itself in multiple logical message types. An ORDERS05 basic type is the foundation for an order (ORDERS), an order change (ORDCHG), an order response (ORDRSP), a quote request (REQOTE) etc.

 

  • EDIDC
    Contains one Control Record per IDoc with one unique IDoc number.
  • EDID4
    Contains one or more Data Records. The reference to the Control Record is done via the unique IDoc number.
  • EDIDS
    Contains one or more Status Records. The reference to the Control Record is done via the unique IDoc number.

 

 

 

Overview

 

 

Material Master  ====MATMAS

Customer Master    ==DEBMAS

Vendor Master    ====CREMAS

Order                        ===ORDERS

Delivery                   ===DESADV

Invoice                     ===INVOIC

 

 

T-Code : WE02/ WE05/WE07/WE09 –To display Idocs

T-Code : BD10 to send Material Master from sender to receiver.

T-Code : BD12 to send Customer Master from sender to receiver.

T-Code : BD14 to send Vendor Master from sender to receiver.

 

 

For Outbound Messages Number Range is 0 to 49

For Inbound Messages Number Range is     50 and above

 

Success message number in Outbound is  03

Success message number in Inbound is 53

Failure message in Inbound is 51

 

T-Code : BD87 –to reprocess the data with the same Idoc No.

T-Code : WE19 –to reprocess the data with different Idoc No.

 

 

 

What is ALE ?

ALE stands for Application Link Enabling. Application Link Enabling is a very common technology used in SAP to transfer master and transactional data between different Logical Systems. ALE is a proprietary SAP technology.

 

What is EDI ?

EDI stands for Electronic Data Interchange. EDI is a much older term and has been used in existence much before SAP. EDI is a generic technology that has been standardized using ANSI and EDIFACT standards. EDI is a very standard Industry standard used for business communication across partners.

What is the Difference between EDI & ALE ?

ALE is used for distributing large volumes of similar data between different Logical systems. For example, distributing customer master data between two SAP systems is easily done using ALE. Similarly, when invoices from the core SAP system needs to be transferred to a different non-SAP invoice processing system, ALE is used.

 

EDI is also used to communicate data between different logical systems. For ex., sending out invoices, or order acknowledgements to customers is a perfect example to use EDI. Similarly, we could use EDI to receive inbound orders from customers.

 

There is a general confusion that ALE is a technology to be used between different SAP systems and EDI is the technology to be used between SAP and non-SAP systems.

 

EDI is used to communicate with Partner Systems, while ALE is used to communicate between Logical Systems. Please do however observe that a Partner System could also be a logical system.

 

Regards,

Kamlesh Rampal.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Test Automation Tool vs. Tricentis Test Automation: A Comparative Analysis
entry.content:
Test automation is crucial for efficient implementation and maintenance of SAP landscapes. This article compares the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Test Automation Tool (TAT) and Tricentis Test Automation (TTA) to help you choose the right tool for your business needs.

Key Differences: TAT vs. TTA

Overview

Feature

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Test Automation Tool (TAT)

Tricentis Test Automation (TTA)

Availability

Pre-installed in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Quality Systems

Requires installation and integration with SAP Solution Manager

SAP Role Requirement

SAP_BR_TEST_ADMIN_AUTOMATION role

SAP Solution Manager user roles

Scope

Focused on SAP S/4HANA Cloud applications

Supports SAP on-premise and cloud products (e.g., Ariba, SuccessFactors)

Pre-built Test Scripts

330+ SAP-delivered automated scripts available

No pre-built scripts; users must create from scratch

API Testing

Limited to specific areas (Finance, Sales, Procurement, Manufacturing)

Supports all APIs

Post-Upgrade Testing

Supported

Not available

Supported UIs

Limited to SAP S/4HANA Cloud UIs

Supports a broad range of UIs (e.g., WebDynpro, GUI, UI5, mobile)

Integration with Non-SAP Systems

Not supported

Supports integration with non-SAP applications

Integration with Cloud ALM

Supported

Planned for future updates

 

Use Cases

Scenario

Recommendation

Focused solely on SAP S/4HANA Cloud

TAT is the best option

Complex integration with other SAP products

TTA is more suitable

Extensive API testing

TTA provides more flexibility

Regression testing for upgrades

TAT supports Post Upgrade Test (PUT) functionality

Integration with non-SAP applications

TTA is the ideal choice

Detailed Feature Comparison

  • Read full article here

Conclusion

Choosing between SAP S/4HANA Cloud Test Automation Tool (TAT) and Tricentis Test Automation (TTA) depends on your organization’s testing scope, integration requirements, and automation needs. For simpler SAP S/4HANA Cloud-focused landscapes, TAT offers a streamlined solution with pre-built scripts and Post Upgrade Testing support. For diverse and complex environments, TTA’s flexibility and wide-ranging integration capabilities make it a powerful choice.

entry.title: How to send Email Notifications only on the Last Retry Attempt in SAP CI/IS when using AEM
entry.content:
Introduction: 

In SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) or Integration Suite, handling retries effectively is crucial for ensuring smooth and reliable message processing. One common requirement in integration scenarios is sending email notifications only when a certain condition is met- such as after the last retry attempt. This helps in reducing notification overload while ensuring that stakeholders are only alerted when necessary.

When using the Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) Sender, you can leverage event driven communication to handle retries in a more controlled manner. In this blog, we will explore how to configure SAP CPI to send email notifications only after the final retry attempt, using the AEM Sender adapter. This solution not only streamlines the notification process but also ensures that notifications are sent only when the retry attempts have been exhausted, making the system more efficient and less intrusive.

Use Case Overview:

In this use case, we will demonstrate how to configure an integration flow using the Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) Sender to consume messages from a queue. The primary goal is to handle retry logic in such a way that an email alert is sent only after the final retry attempt is reached, ensuring notifications are triggered only when necessary.

Integration Flow:

Below is a sample Integration Flow created to route email alert only on the last retry attempt.

 

Important note: Make sure to use * in the Allowed headers as shown, to ensure that the full set of headers is received from the AEM sender.

 

 

 

 

 

Now, let's discuss the basic configuration.

For guidance on configuring the AEM sender channel, refer to SAP help or other relevant blogs. In this example, we have set the Maximum Message Processing Attempts to 3, and our goal is to send an alert only on the third (final) retry attempt.

This is achieved by using the "LocalProcessingAttempt" header, which is dynamically set during runtime based on the number of attempts. Initially, the AEM sender sets this header to 1 as shown below, and it increments with each subsequent retry.

In the local sub-process, we use a router to define the routing condition as ${header.LocalProcessingAttempt} > '2'. This means that once the attempt exceeds 2, the flow will follow the path where the email receiver is configured.

Note: To make the retry attempt dynamic, you can substitute the value 2 with a property that we can set in a content modifier before router.

Conclusion:

 With the use of the "LocalProcessingAttempt" header and routing conditions, you can tailor the retry logic to suit specific business needs and enhance the overall efficiency of your integration processes.By configuring the integration flow to send email alerts only on the final retry attempt, you can significantly reduce unnecessary notifications and focus attention on the most critical events. This method provides better control over communication in your SAP CPI environment, ensuring that stakeholders are only alerted when all retry options have been exhausted.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA ITSMobile Visual Editor Freezes at "loading"
entry.content:
Hi,

In ths blog post, I would like to share a solution to the problem of opening ITSMobile screen template via Visual Editor.

Symptom

  • Execute transaction code SE80.
  • In the Repository Browser, select "Internet Service" item in the dropdown box.
  • In the input field, type the service name and press ENTER.
  • Navigate to a template and double-click it. In the below example, it is give the your template.
  • The Editor freezes, not showing the actual content

 

Solution for Template Editing Issue in ITSMobile Application on S/4HANA 2023

When trying to open the template for editing in the Visual Editor of the ITSMobile application in S/4HANA 2023, the screen seems to be loading but ends up freezing. Below, I share the solution for this issue.

Upon reviewing notes 2696538 and 2570160, the first suggested solution is to set the user parameter "W3_XML_EDIT_DEV_CONF = ABAPDelay=1000" in the SAP system. If the template still does not open after setting this parameter, it is necessary to check the "icf/user_recheck" parameter through transaction RZ11. The parameter description is provided below.

Parameter Description
The parameter defines if a new logon data check and addressing of an existing session is to take place for stateful HTTP communication or not. The authentication data needs to match the data held in the session.

0 = If the check is inactive

1 = If the check is active

SU01 --> Change --> Parameters Tab

 

RZ11

Additional Information
Currently, the Visual Editor does not support Microsoft Edge. The details are shared below.

It has been mentioned in SAP note 3191851 that even if Microsoft Edge is selected instead of Internet Explorer in the SAP GUI settings, support for Edge is currently unavailable. As stated in the note, ongoing checks are being performed to enable Edge to work properly.

SAP GUI Options

That is all. I hope that is useful for you.

Thanks for reading.

entry.title: Converting Rows to Columns in a CDS Report in S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, it is common to encounter scenarios where different types of data are stored in the same column across multiple rows. To meet reporting requirements, this data often needs to be displayed in separate columns on the same row. This article demonstrates how to achieve this transformation using Custom CDS Views.

Example Scenario: Tax Data Conversion

Consider a table where various tax components (e.g., Total Amount, Base Amount, IGST, CGST, SGST) are stored in a single column, AmountinCoCodeCurrency, but on different rows. The goal is to display these values in distinct columns in the same row for a CDS report.

Input Table

Company Code

Journal Entry

Fiscal Year

Posting View Item

AmountinCoCodeCurrency

Line Item ID

Transaction Key

Financial Account Type

1110

94000001

2024

1

1250

 

 

K

1110

94000001

2024

2

1050

S

 

 

1110

94000001

2024

3

100

T

JIS

 

1110

94000001

2024

4

100

T

JIC

 

1110

94000001

2024

5

0

T

JII

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Output Table


Company Code

Journal Entry

Fiscal Year

Total Amount

Base Amount

IGST

CGST

SGST

1110

94000001

2024

1250

1050

100

100

0

 

Steps to Achieve the Transformation

  1. Create Custom CDS Views for Each Tax Component

For each tax component, create a Custom CDS View to extract its specific data. Use a unique filter condition to isolate the relevant rows.

  • Base Amount:
    Filter: Line Item ID = 'S' and Financial Account Type != 'K'.
  • Total Amount:
    Filter: Financial Account Type = 'K'.
  • IGST:
    Filter: Transaction Key = 'JIS'.
  • CGST:
    Filter: Transaction Key = 'JIC'.
  • SGST:
    Filter: Transaction Key = 'JII'.
  1. Combine CDS Views

After creating individual CDS Views for each tax component, combine them into a single Custom CDS View using an Inner Join. Use common fields such as Company Code, Journal Entry, and Fiscal Year as the join conditions to align the data.

  1. Define the Output Structure

The resulting CDS View should include columns for:

  • Company Code
  • Journal Entry
  • Fiscal Year
  • Total Amount
  • Base Amount
  • IGST
  • CGST
  • SGST

Key Considerations

  • Filter Design: Ensure that each tax component has a unique identifying filter to avoid overlapping or missing data.
  • Join Conditions: Use precise join conditions to maintain data integrity and avoid duplication.
  • Performance: Test the combined CDS View for performance, especially with large datasets. Optimize as needed.

By following this approach, you can efficiently transform row-based data into columnar format in a CDS Report, as illustrated in the example. This method provides flexibility for customizing reports and meeting business requirements in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

entry.content:
Hi team,

We're part of S4HANA Migration Project where the ask is to migrate legacy transaction data to S4HANA. However the challenge with us that we have to migrate even monthly transactional data for -

a) Balance Sheet GL Transactions (actuals)

b) PnL GL Transactions (actuals)

entry.title: Is it possible to generate a customer clearing document without items in transaction FB05?
entry.content: We have a backlog of customer PA's and we are using the 'POSTING_INTERFACE_CLEARING' Fm for mass clearing, however, I would like to know if there is any customization so that the generated documents do not create items, since we do not have difference postings.

entry.title: Re: How to load legacy financial transactional data to S4HANA
entry.content:
Hi, @amit_2024!

From the Migration Cockpit's perspective, you could check the available Migration Objects of the app in the official Help Portal page:

  • Available Migration Objects | SAP Help Portal

It's important to mention though that the Migration Cockpit can't used constantly (as monthly) for data uploads/updates, as it's meant to be used as the initial data load performed in the system, explained in KBA below:

  • 2684818 - SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit usage for mass processing data or as interface

I'd like to emphasize a few phrases of the KBA above:

  • The SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit tool and content is not build to be used as a kind of mass processing app to change or update existing data.
  • You should use the provided interfaces or mass processing apps of the business object application area instead. For more information about maintenance apps, check the SAP Fiori Library.

Considering this, the FI and G/L experts of the community might be able to further help you with your current business requirements!

Best regards,
Caio

entry.title: Re: How to load legacy financial transactional data to S4HANA
entry.content: Thanks Caio for swift response. There are migration objects available like FI - Accounts Payable Open Items(new G/L), FI - Historical balance etc. but there are no standard migration objects available for Balance Sheet GL Transactions (daily) and PnL GL Transactions(daily). If you come across of such requirements then please let me know

Introdução ao SAP Fiori: Um Guia para Iniciantes em Desenvolvimento
entry.content:
Descubra como eu, um iniciante em desenvolvimento SAP ABAP/FIORI o essencial sobre o assunto e comece sua jornada no desenvolvimento de aplicações empresariais modernas e amigáveis.

Vamos lá!

SAP Fiori é uma linguagem de design e uma estrutura de experiência do usuário (UX) que revoluciona a maneira como as aplicações empresariais são construídas e utilizadas. Desenvolvido pela SAP, ele fornece uma interface consistente e intuitiva para vários processos de negócios, aumentando a produtividade e a satisfação dos usuários. Para iniciantes que desejam se aprofundar no desenvolvimento do SAP Fiori, é crucial entender seus princípios e arquitetura básicos.

O que é o SAP Fiori?

SAP Fiori é uma coleção de aplicações utilizadas em funções empresariais como finanças, RH e manufatura. Essas aplicações são baseadas em funções, o que significa que elas fornecem as informações corretas no momento certo, simplificando tarefas complexas. As aplicações Fiori são projetadas para funcionar perfeitamente em diferentes dispositivos - desktop, tablet e smartphone.

Principais Características:

  1. Design Responsivo: As aplicações Fiori se adaptam a diferentes tamanhos de tela, garantindo uma experiência de usuário perfeita em todos os dispositivos.

  2. Acesso Baseado em Função: Os usuários vêem apenas o que precisam, com base em suas funções, aumentando a eficiência.

  3. Simples e Intuitivo: As aplicações Fiori seguem uma filosofia de design simples, tornando-as fáceis de usar e navegar.

Começando com o Desenvolvimento SAP Fiori

Para começar a desenvolver com SAP Fiori, é necessário entender a arquitetura e as ferramentas subjacentes. As aplicações Fiori são tipicamente construídas usando SAPUI5, uma estrutura JavaScript fornecida pela SAP, que suporta princípios de design web responsivo.

Passos Básicos no Desenvolvimento Fiori:

  1. Configure seu Ambiente: Instale ferramentas necessárias como o SAP Web IDE ou BAS (Business Application Studio).

  2. Aprenda SAPUI5: Familiarize-se com a estrutura SAPUI5 e seus controles.

  3. Entenda os Serviços OData: As aplicações Fiori consomem dados através de serviços OData. Entender como criar e usar esses serviços é essencial.

  4. Construa sua Primeira Aplicação: Comece criando uma aplicação simples, incorporando gradualmente funcionalidades mais complexas.

Recursos:

  • SAP Learning Hub: Oferece uma ampla gama de tutoriais e cursos.

  • Fóruns aqui da Comunidade: Engaje-se com outros desenvolvedores para compartilhar conhecimentos e solucionar problemas.

Começar com o desenvolvimento do SAP Fiori pode parecer desafiador no início, mas pelo que percebi, com os recursos certos e uma abordagem de aprendizado estruturada, podemos criar aplicações impactantes que transformam as operações empresariais.
